      the 6-space-overflow flag layouts, strips `(choices: …)`/`(default: …)`/`preset:` into fields.
- [x] Recursion helper: walk subcommands via `claude <path…> --help`; **depth-capped**, skip the `help`
      pseudo-command, per-call **timeout**, tolerant fallback (degrade to a minimal node, never raise,
      when a call fails or a sub-section doesn't parse). → `introspect_cli()`; command-head validation
      drops embedded example blocks (e.g. the `mcp add` `Examples:` block) from the subcommand list.
- [x] Refactor `check-version-drift` onto the shared parser (no behaviour change; its command-list diff
      still works — verified "command list unchanged vs snapshot (11 commands)"). `make check` green.

### 8.2 Generator + machine artifact  [R16.AC1/AC3/AC4/AC6; §12.1–12.4]  ✅
- [x] `meta/cli-reference.schema.json` — JSON Schema for the artifact (root command tree +
      `supplement_sections`; **`source` + `provenance` required on every entry** — command, flag,
      argument, and supplement entry; recursive `command` def via `$ref`).
- [x] `meta/cli-reference-supplement.yaml` — authored doc-only surface (in-REPL slash commands, output
      styles), grouped into titled sections, **section `provenance: <doc URL> (retrieved <date>)`**
      inherited onto entries. Seeded from official docs (WebFetch of `code.claude.com/docs/en/commands`
      + `/output-styles`, retrieved 2026-05-31); excludes `/vim`/`/pr-comments` (removed before the
      installed CLI). Nothing from memory (R12.AC3).
- [x] `tools/render-cli-reference --generate` — recursive introspection ∪ supplement →
      `meta/cli-reference.json`. **Byte-stable** emission (`json.dumps(indent=2, ensure_ascii=False,
      sort_keys=True)`, list order preserved, **no generated-date**); `cli_version` from `claude --version`.
- [x] Generated against installed **2.1.159** (CLI drifted 2.1.158→2.1.159; generated against the real
      installed CLI per R12.AC3). 43 commands + 97 supplement entries; **byte-stable** (identical on
      regenerate); validates vs schema; provenance present on every entry. _Commit pending review._

### 8.3 Human render  [R16.AC2; §12.5]  ✅
- [x] `tools/render-cli-reference --render` — `cli-reference.json` → `course/reference/cli-reference.md`:
      generated-banner with `cli_version` in **text**; TOC; nested command sections (heading depth =
      command depth) with **flag tables** (choices/default folded in as structured annotations, pipes
      escaped); supplement sections with doc-URL provenance as inline links; GitHub-style anchors.
      `--all` (generate+render) added. Render is **deterministic** (identical on re-render — the basis
      for 8.4's drift gate). Page passes `check-links`.
- [x] Generated the first page (732 lines); **linked** from the top `README.md` (structure table) and
      `course/units/README.md` index (via `render-index`, so it can't drift). `make check` green. _Commit
      pending review._

### 8.4 Gates: `--check` / `--all` + suite wiring  [R16.AC6; §12.1, §12.8]  ✅
- [x] `--all` (generate→render, added in 8.3); `--check` *default* (offline: schema-validate — which
      enforces source+provenance on every entry — + re-render and diff vs committed md); `--check --cli`
      (re-introspect + diff the json — machine freshness, degrades to PENDING when the CLI is absent).
- [x] Wired `render-cli-reference --check` into `make check` + `make check-strict` (new `cli-reference`
      target) — which the `check-on-edit` hook and the CI `checks` job both run, so all three layers are
      covered. Wired `--check --cli` into `make drift` and the CI **scheduled** `version-drift` job
      (claude-guarded), **not** every CI run (offline-safe). `make render` now also re-renders the page.
- [x] Verified: tampering the json without re-rendering → `--check` **fails** (page out of sync);
      re-render → **passes**; offline (no `claude` on PATH) → `--check` still gates the page and
      `--check --cli` PENDs gracefully (exit 0). `make check` + `make drift` green.

### 8.5 Changelog digest + check + learner "What's new" + inline `added_in` markers  [R17, R16; §12.5/§12.6]  ✅
- [x] `meta/version-changelog.md` — a **2.1.158 baseline** entry **plus** a real `## 2.1.158 → 2.1.159`
      entry from the official changelog (`github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md`,
      retrieved 2026-05-31): 2.1.159 = "internal infrastructure improvements (no user-facing changes),"
      `_Course impact:_ none`. Header documents the cumulative-on-refresh format + provenance discipline.
- [x] `tools/check-version-changelog` (R17.AC5) — asserts the recorded `_verified_version` (2.1.158) has
      a matching digest entry, matching the heading **version-subject** (before the `(`) so a version
      merely *mentioned* in prose doesn't satisfy it. Wired into `make check` / `check-strict`
      (`changelog` target). Verified pass + fail.
- [x] **Learner "What's new" surface (P8-r17-surface):** `render-cli-reference --render` adds a
      **"What's new" section** atop the page (latest digest entry, `{{vd:key}}`-resolved + fence-aware,
      + a link to the full digest) and a TOC entry. Deterministic; passes `check-links`/`check-version-refs`.
- [x] **Inline new-option markers (`added_in`, P8-added-in):** optional `added_in` in the schema
      (command + flag); `--generate` delta-marks vs the previously-committed json (carry-forward,
      omit-when-null, byte-stable); `--render` shows `🆕 _new in <ver>_` inline. Verified: regenerate
      @2.1.159 ⇒ unchanged/no markers; a synthetic older prior-json ⇒ `--effort` stamped + marker rendered.
- [x] Added the standing R12.AC7 refresh steps to `meta/version-record.md`: regenerate via
      `render-cli-reference --all` + record the cumulative `version-changelog.md` entry.

### 8.6 Dogfood pointers  [R16.AC7, R14.AC2; §12.9]
- [ ] U4 `unit.src.md` — a light **titled** pointer to `meta/cli-reference.json` as the exhaustive
      single-source sibling of `version-data.yaml` (no bare codes, per P7 house style); `make render`.
- [ ] U10 `unit.src.md` — a light callout that this reference was built **spec-driven** (R16/R17 → §12 →
      this P8 file → implementation); `make render`.
- [ ] Eyeball both rendered `unit.md`; confirm reading-time unaffected; `make check` green.

### 8.7 Resilience: dynamic requirement discovery  [R13.AC5; §12.8]  ⟵ run after 8.2–8.4, 8.6
- [ ] `check-traceability` — **discover the requirement set from `requirements.md`** (`### Rn` headings),
      replacing the hardcoded `R1–R15` regex/range. Keep the **can-do set closed** (`C1–C17+CV`).
- [ ] Audit the other checks (`check-version-refs`, …) for hardcoded `R#` ranges; same treatment where
      found.
- [ ] Verify R16 + R17 are now discovered **and referenced** (the new tools' docstrings + the reference
      page + the U4/U10 pointers); `make check` green with the generalized check.

### 8.8 Maintainer-guide playbook  [R13.AC3; §12.9]
- [ ] `course/maintainer-guide.md` — new **"Adding a post-v1 enhancement"** subsection codifying the
      additive pattern: new requirement → new `tasks/P{N}` file → `decisions.md` entry + 🔓 ledger →
      *generalized* (not hardcoded) enforcement. Fold in the R16 regenerate + R17 changelog refresh steps.

### 8.9 Close-out  [continuity hygiene]
- [ ] `make check` green; add the **P8 index row + status header** to `tasks.md`; mark `IMPLEMENTATION.md`
      §3 (design → tasks → complete); update `decisions.md` P8 entries + the 🔓 ledger (log the
      **`cli-commands.snapshot` ⊂ `cli-reference.json`** fold as a non-blocking open-loop).
- [ ] Final commit(s) in slices on `feat/cli-reference`; open PR / merge to `main` — **ask before
      push/merge** (CLAUDE.md).

## Locked decisions (from the design gate — do not re-litigate)

- **R16 scope frozen** — no new can-do, lab, or coverage area (`check-coverage` + traceability part B
  untouched). Future enhancements get their own requirement + phase.
- **One tool, four modes** — expensive recursive `claude --help` introspection runs **only** in
  `--generate`/`--all`; `--render`/`--check` are pure/offline (never re-introspect).
- **Byte-stable machine artifact** — `cli_version` in the json, **generated-date in `version-record.md`**.
- **Resilience over hardcoding** — requirement enumeration is discovered dynamically; only the requirement
  set grows (can-dos stay the closed `C1–C17+CV`).

## Traceability (this phase)

| Req | Task(s) |
|---|---|
| R16.AC1 | 8.2 |
| R16.AC2 | 8.3 |
| R16.AC3 | 8.2 (introspected provenance), 8.5 |
| R16.AC4 | 8.2 (supplement union) |
| R16.AC5 | 8.4 (`--all`), 8.5 (refresh steps), `version-record.md` |
| R16.AC6 | 8.2 (byte-stable), 8.4 (regenerate-and-diff) |
| R16.AC7 | 8.6 |
| R17.AC1–AC4 | 8.5 |
| R17.AC5 | 8.5 (`check-version-changelog`) |
| R17.AC6 | 8.5, 8.8 |
| R13.AC5 | 8.7 (dynamic discovery) |
| R13.AC3 | 8.8 (playbook) |

content.text:
Context primed. (Note: that first burst had duplicate tool calls — my error; results all came back on the second pass.)

## Where we are

**P8 — Version-resilience enhancements** (post-v1, **not release-blocking**), on branch `feat/cli-reference`. v1 (P1–P7) is complete and on `main`. P8 adds two approved requirements:
- **R16** — exhaustive, generated CLI reference (`tools/render-cli-reference`, 4 modes)
- **R17** — per-version changelog digest captured on refresh

**Task progress** (`tasks/P8-cli-reference.md`):
- ✅ **8.1–8.5 done** — help parser in `_common.py`; generator + `meta/cli-reference.json` (byte-stable, 43 cmds + 97 supplement entries); human render → `course/reference/cli-reference.md`; `--check`/`--all` gates wired into `make check`/`drift`/CI; changelog digest + `check-version-changelog` + learner "What's new" + inline `added_in` markers. All committed (last commit `5151a64` captures the session through 8.5).
- ⬜ **8.6** — dogfood pointers in U4 (`cli-reference.json` as single-source sibling) and U10 (built spec-driven) `unit.src.md`
- ⬜ **8.7** — resilience: make `check-traceability` **discover requirements dynamically** from `requirements.md` (drop hardcoded R1–R15). **Must run after 8.6** or `make check` fails mid-build (the ordering constraint).
- ⬜ **8.8** — maintainer-guide "Adding a post-v1 enhancement" playbook
- ⬜ **8.9** — close-out (tasks.md row, §3 update, decisions + 🔓 ledger, final commits, **ask before push/merge**)

## Open loops worth noting
- **Version drift, deliberately unbumped:** installed CLI moved **2.1.158 → 2.1.159** during the build. `cli-reference.json` was generated against the *real* 2.1.159 (per R12.AC3), but per decision **P8-no-bump** `version-record.md` still records 2.1.158 — not bumped now, by your call.
- **L1** (version debt): `ci` + `managed-settings` keys remain `unverified` (no GitHub Actions / enterprise access here).
- Non-blocking fold to log at close-out: `cli-commands.snapshot` ⊂ `cli-reference.json`.

## Working state
Branch `feat/cli-reference`, **clean tree**, up to date with origin. Mid-flight: P8 execution paused after 8.5.

## Next action
Resume at **8.6 — dogfood pointers** (U4 + U10 `unit.src.md`, then `make render`), which unblocks 8.7's dynamic-traceability switch. Want me to start on 8.6?

[‹ Claude Code Mastery](../../README.md)

# Course units

The 16 units, in the default order. Each is self-contained; skim a unit's **Skip-check** and skip deliberately if you already know it. Track yourself on the [progress checklist](../progress-checklist.md); finish with the [capstone](../capstone/). Need a flag or command? See the generated [CLI reference](../reference/cli-reference.md).

## First Wins

| # | Unit | Read | Lab |
|---|------|------|-----|
| 1 | [Set up Claude Code and make your first verified change](./01-onboarding-first-win/unit.md) | 8 min | 15 min |
| 2 | [Explore and explain an unfamiliar codebase](./02-explore-a-codebase/unit.md) | 8 min | 20 min |
| 3 | [Operate safely while letting Claude act on your code](./03-operate-safely/unit.md) | 10 min | 20 min |
| 4 | [Steer Claude with project memory and deliberate context](./04-memory-and-context/unit.md) | 9 min | 18 min |

## Daily Driver

| # | Unit | Read | Lab |
|---|------|------|-----|
| 5 | [Ship a feature end-to-end with explore → plan → code → commit](./05-ship-a-feature/unit.md) | 10 min | 25 min |
| 6 | [Drive a new behavior test-first (red → green)](./06-tdd/unit.md) | 9 min | 30 min |
| 7 | [Debug an unfamiliar failure by confirming the root cause](./07-debug-a-failure/unit.md) | 9 min | 30 min |
| 8 | [Turn a body of work into clean commits and a reviewable PR](./08-git-and-pr/unit.md) | 9 min | 25 min |

## Force Multiplier

| # | Unit | Read | Lab |
|---|------|------|-----|
| 9 | [Onboard a legacy codebase and refactor it without changing behavior](./09-onboard-refactor-legacy/unit.md) | 11 min | 40 min |
| 10 | [Build a feature spec-first: requirements → design → tasks](./10-spec-driven-dev/unit.md) | 11 min | 35 min |
| 11 | [Review a change for correctness and security — and triage the findings](./11-code-and-security-review/unit.md) | 11 min | 35 min |

## Autonomy & Scale

| # | Unit | Read | Lab |
|---|------|------|-----|
| 12 | [Package a repeatable routine as a custom command and a skill](./12-commands-and-skills/unit.md) | 10 min | 20 min |
| 13 | [Delegate a scoped task to a subagent](./13-subagents/unit.md) | 12 min | 20 min |

1	<!-- SOURCE — authored unit. Edit THIS file; the sibling unit.md is generated by
2	     tools/render-units, which resolves the version-data tokens. Run `make render` after editing. -->
3	---
4	id: U4
5	title: "Steer Claude with project memory and deliberate context"
6	stage: first-wins
7	depth_tier: core
8	use_case: "Make Claude reliably follow my project's conventions"
9	can_do: [C5]
10	workflows: []
11	coverage_areas: [7, 8, 26, 28]
12	prerequisites: [U1]
13	reading_time_min: 9
14	lab_time_min: 18
15	---
16	
17	# Steer Claude with project memory and deliberate context
18	
19	## Learning objectives
20	
21	By the end of this unit you can:
22	
23	- **Write and place a `CLAUDE.md` that measurably changes how Claude behaves on a task** — encode a
24	  project's conventions once and have Claude follow them.
25	- **Treat the context window as a managed resource** — inspect what's loaded, select what's relevant,
26	  and clear what isn't.
27	- **Locate where settings live** (project vs user) and recognize that the permission rules from **Operate safely**
28	  are configured here.
29	- **Verify that your memory/context change actually took effect** before trusting the behavior change —
30	  the cross-cutting verification (CV) reflex applied to configuration.
31	
32	## Fast path (TL;DR)
33	
34	> Two levers steer Claude beyond the words you type. **(1) Project memory** — a short, true
35	> `CLAUDE.md` that's auto-loaded so Claude knows your conventions without being told each time.
36	> **(2) Context** — the finite window of what Claude can currently see; more isn't better, *relevant*
37	> is. Inspect it, select into it (`@`-mentions, from **Explore a codebase**), and clear it when you switch tasks. The lab
38	> has you change one line of `CLAUDE.md` and watch Claude's answer change — then confirm via the
39	> context inspector that your file is actually loaded.
40	
41	## Skip-check
42	
43	**Skip this unit if you can already:** write a `CLAUDE.md` that reliably makes Claude follow your
44	project's conventions, place it where it'll actually load (and confirm it did), and manage the
45	context window deliberately — selecting what's relevant and clearing what isn't — rather than hoping
46	more context helps.
47	
48	## Concept
49	
50	You've now driven a change, explored code, and operated safely. What you haven't done
51	yet is make Claude *reliably yours* — follow your conventions without being re-told every session.
52	That's two distinct levers, and conflating them is the usual mistake: **memory** is what Claude knows
53	by default; **context** is what Claude can see right now.
54	
55	**1 — Project memory (`CLAUDE.md`).** {{vd:memory}} A `CLAUDE.md` is a file Claude auto-discovers and
56	loads as standing instructions — your project's conventions, architecture, and gotchas, stated once.
57	The baseline you were handed in your first unit (`codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md`) is a working example;
58	this unit is where you learn to *shape* one. The principles are what matter, and they're version-
59	independent:
60	
61	- **Short and true beats long and aspirational.** Memory is loaded into every session's context, so
62	  every line costs attention and tokens. A tight file of real conventions outperforms an essay. A
63	  line that's *false* (says tests are `pytest` after they moved) is worse than no line — it actively
64	  misleads. **Treat `CLAUDE.md` like code:** keep it current, or delete the stale part.
65	- **Encode decisions, not documentation.** "New domain logic goes in `app/services/`, not routers" is
66	  worth a line because it changes what Claude *does*. A restatement of what the framework already
67	  makes obvious is not.
68	- **Memory is layered.** Project memory (committed, shared with the team) is distinct from your
69	  personal/user memory (your machine, your habits). Put team conventions in the project file so
70	  everyone — and every session — gets them.
71	
72	> **This course dogfoods it:** the repo's own root
73	> [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) *is* the project memory used to build this course, and it's
74	> deliberately short — what the repo is, how it's organized, the working agreements. Read it as a
75	> real specimen, not a toy.
76	
77	**2 — Context is a finite, managed resource.** The context window is everything Claude can see at this
78	moment: the system prompt, your `CLAUDE.md`, the files and output pulled in this session, and the
79	conversation so far. Two consequences:
80	
81	- **More context is not better context.** Dumping the whole repo "to be safe" crowds out the few
82	  files that matter and degrades answers. **Select deliberately:** anchor with `@`-mentions,
83	  start from the right directory, and don't drag in what the task doesn't need.
84	- **Inspect and reset it.** {{vd:context-cmds}} You can see what's currently loaded, compact a long
85	  session, or clear it to start clean when you switch tasks. A session that's been wandering for an
86	  hour is carrying a lot of irrelevant context — clearing it is often the fastest fix for "Claude
87	  seems confused."
88	
89	**3 — Settings (`settings.json`) — where config (incl. permissions) lives.** {{vd:settings}} The
90	permission allow/deny rules you *chose* postures for in **Operate safely** are *configured* here. The baseline
91	[`.claude/settings.json`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/.claude/settings.json) in the
92	primary codebase auto-approves a few safe, routine commands — running the test suite, the DB seed
93	script, and the dev server — so lab work doesn't prompt on every step, while anything outside that
94	allow-list still goes through the permission gate. Settings resolve from more than one source
95	(project vs user); project settings are committed and shared, so put team-wide rules there.
96	*(Enterprise/managed settings can override these — see **Operate safely**.)*
97	
98	> **Awareness — output styles.** Claude's response style/verbosity is itself configurable:
99	> {{vd:output-styles}} Useful to know it exists; not something this course drills.
100	
101	**4 — Verify the change took effect (CV).** Memory and context edits are *changes*, and the unit's
102	through-line applies: don't assume, confirm. The classic failure is editing the *wrong* `CLAUDE.md`
103	(your user file instead of the project file, or the wrong repo) so it never loads — and then puzzling
104	over why Claude "ignored" it. Before you trust a behavior change, **inspect the context to confirm
105	your file is actually loaded.** That 10-second check is the difference between steering Claude and
106	imagining you are.
107	
108	**Version currency.** This unit was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`; the
109	context-management commands are in-REPL and may shift between releases — confirm them with `/help` and
110	see [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) if your version differs.
111	
112	## Worked example
113	
114	The repo's root [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) is the authentic specimen. Notice what it
115	does and doesn't do:
116	
117	- It states **what the repo is** and **how it's organized** in a few lines, then points at the
118	  single-source-of-truth files (`meta/*`, the spec) rather than restating them — *"cross-cutting
119	  facts live once… reference it; don't inline."* That's context discipline encoded as memory.
120	- It records **working agreements** that change behavior ("never author a version-specific value from
121	  memory"; "keep state current") — decisions, not documentation.
122	- It's short. Every line earns its place in every session's context.
123	
124	The codebase baseline ([`taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md))
125	is the same idea scoped to one app: what it is, where logic lives, the services→exceptions→main
126	convention, how to run tests. When you ask Claude "where does this new logic go?", *that* file is why
127	it answers "the service layer" instead of guessing.
128	
129	## Lab
130	
131	**Goal:** prove to yourself that project memory **measurably** changes Claude's behavior — by changing
132	one line of `CLAUDE.md` and watching the answer change — and confirm the file is actually loaded.
133	
134	**Starting state:** the clean primary codebase with its baseline `CLAUDE.md`
135	(`codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`). This lab makes a small, reversible edit to that `CLAUDE.md`;
136	restore it at the end with `git restore codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md` (there's no code
137	change to reset).
138	
139	**Steps:**
140	
141	1. `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude` (plan mode is fine — this lab doesn't need to write
142	   code).
143	2. **Baseline behavior.** Ask: *"I want to add validation that a project's name can't be empty. Which
144	   layer does that belong in, and how should the error reach the client?"* A good answer cites the
145	   **service layer** + a **domain exception** mapped in `main.py` — because the baseline `CLAUDE.md`
146	   says so. Note how specific the answer is.
147	3. **Confirm the file is loaded (CV).** Inspect the loaded context and verify the
148	   project `CLAUDE.md` is actually present. This is the check most people skip.
149	4. **Change the memory.** Add one concrete, checkable convention to `CLAUDE.md`, e.g. under
150	   *Conventions*: *"Validation failures must raise the project's domain `ValidationError`; never raise
151	   `HTTPException` from a service."* Save it.
152	5. **Observe the behavior change.** Re-ask a related question (*"how should I signal a validation
153	   failure from a service?"*). Claude's answer now reflects your new rule. That shift — caused by one
154	   line you wrote — is the whole point of the unit.
155	6. **A/B to be sure (optional).** Temporarily comment the line out (or `/clear` and ask in a fresh
156	   session without it); the specific guidance disappears. Restore the file.
157	
158	**Self-check (objective):** you pass if **(a)** you can point to the exact line in `CLAUDE.md` that
159	caused Claude's answer to change between steps 2/5 (and 6), and **(b)** you confirmed via the context
160	inspector (step 3) that the project `CLAUDE.md` was loaded. If the answer *didn't* change, the file
161	almost certainly isn't being loaded — recheck step 3 before concluding "memory doesn't work."
162	
163	**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch — the lab's artifact is a *behavior change*, not a diff
164	(your `CLAUDE.md` edit is reverted at the end). The reference is the reasoning above: a specific,
165	true, behavior-changing line, confirmed loaded.
166	
167	**Verify (CV):** the load-bearing habit here is step 3 — **never trust that a `CLAUDE.md` edit took
168	effect without inspecting the context.** Editing the wrong file (user vs project) is the #1 reason
169	memory "doesn't work," and it's invisible unless you look.
170	
171	## Common pitfalls
172	
173	- **Editing the wrong `CLAUDE.md`.** Project vs user memory are different files; a change to one won't
174	  show up if Claude is loading the other. Confirm with the context inspector, not by faith.
175	- **Writing an essay.** A long `CLAUDE.md` dilutes the conventions that matter and burns context every
176	  session. Short, imperative, true. Cut anything the framework already makes obvious.
177	- **Letting memory go stale.** A `CLAUDE.md` that describes how the project *used* to work actively
178	  misleads Claude. Update it in the same change that breaks its assumptions — treat it like code.
179	- **Hoarding context.** Loading more files "to be safe" makes answers worse, not safer. Select what's
180	  relevant; `/clear` between unrelated tasks instead of letting one session accrete.
181	- **Assuming the change worked.** A behavior that didn't change usually means the file didn't load —
182	  diagnose that first, don't keep rewording the convention.
183	
184	## Going deeper
185	
186	- **Next:** **Ship a feature** puts this to work — a sharp `CLAUDE.md` plus plan mode is what makes
187	  the explore→plan→code→commit loop reliable. Everything from here assumes you can steer Claude with
188	  memory and context.
189	- [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) (this repo's own) and [`meta/`](../../../meta/) — the living
190	  example of "facts live once, referenced not duplicated."
191	- The `@`-mention selection habit from [Explore a codebase](../02-explore-a-codebase/unit.md) is the
192	  context lever in miniature; the permission *postures* from [Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md)
193	  are configured in the `settings.json` discussed here.
194	- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where context/memory version-specifics
195	  are recorded if your CLI differs.
196	- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
197	  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
198	</content>
199

  are configured here.

  the cross-cutting verification (CV) reflex applied to configuration.

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> Two levers steer Claude beyond the words you type. **(1) Project memory** — a short, true

more context helps.

yet is make Claude *reliably yours* — follow your conventions without being re-told every session.

> deliberately short — what the repo is, how it's organized, the working agreements. Read it as a

**2 — Context is a finite, managed resource.** The context window is everything Claude can see at this

- **More context is not better context.** Dumping the whole repo "to be safe" crowds out the few

**3 — Settings (`settings.json`) — where config (incl. permissions) lives.** {{vd:settings}} The

it answers "the service layer" instead of guessing.

## Lab

**Goal:** prove to yourself that project memory **measurably** changes Claude's behavior — by changing
one line of `CLAUDE.md` and watching the answer change — and confirm the file is actually loaded.

**Starting state:** the clean primary codebase with its baseline `CLAUDE.md`
(`codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`). This lab makes a small, reversible edit to that `CLAUDE.md`;
restore it at the end with `git restore codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md` (there's no code
change to reset).

**Steps:**

1. `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude` (plan mode is fine — this lab doesn't need to write
   code).
2. **Baseline behavior.** Ask: *"I want to add validation that a project's name can't be empty. Which
   layer does that belong in, and how should the error reach the client?"* A good answer cites the
   **service layer** + a **domain exception** mapped in `main.py` — because the baseline `CLAUDE.md`
   says so. Note how specific the answer is.
3. **Confirm the file is loaded (CV).** Inspect the loaded context and verify the
   project `CLAUDE.md` is actually present. This is the check most people skip.
4. **Change the memory.** Add one concrete, checkable convention to `CLAUDE.md`, e.g. under
   *Conventions*: *"Validation failures must raise the project's domain `ValidationError`; never raise
   `HTTPException` from a service."* Save it.
5. **Observe the behavior change.** Re-ask a related question (*"how should I signal a validation
   failure from a service?"*). Claude's answer now reflects your new rule. That shift — caused by one
   line you wrote — is the whole point of the unit.
6. **A/B to be sure (optional).** Temporarily comment the line out (or `/clear` and ask in a fresh
   session without it); the specific guidance disappears. Restore the file.

**Self-check (objective):** you pass if **(a)** you can point to the exact line in `CLAUDE.md` that
caused Claude's answer to change between steps 2/5 (and 6), and **(b)** you confirmed via the context
inspector (step 3) that the project `CLAUDE.md` was loaded. If the answer *didn't* change, the file
almost certainly isn't being loaded — recheck step 3 before concluding "memory doesn't work."

**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch — the lab's artifact is a *behavior change*, not a diff
(your `CLAUDE.md` edit is reverted at the end). The reference is the reasoning above: a specific,
true, behavior-changing line, confirmed loaded.

**Verify (CV):** the load-bearing habit here is step 3 — **never trust that a `CLAUDE.md` edit took
effect without inspecting the context.** Editing the wrong file (user vs project) is the #1 reason
memory "doesn't work," and it's invisible unless you look.

## Common pitfalls

- **Editing the wrong `CLAUDE.md`.** Project vs user memory are different files; a change to one won't
  show up if Claude is loading the other. Confirm with the context inspector, not by faith.
- **Writing an essay.** A long `CLAUDE.md` dilutes the conventions that matter and burns context every
  session. Short, imperative, true. Cut anything the framework already makes obvious.
- **Letting memory go stale.** A `CLAUDE.md` that describes how the project *used* to work actively
  misleads Claude. Update it in the same change that breaks its assumptions — treat it like code.
- **Hoarding context.** Loading more files "to be safe" makes answers worse, not safer. Select what's
  relevant; `/clear` between unrelated tasks instead of letting one session accrete.
- **Assuming the change worked.** A behavior that didn't change usually means the file didn't load —
  diagnose that first, don't keep rewording the convention.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** **Ship a feature** puts this to work — a sharp `CLAUDE.md` plus plan mode is what makes
  the explore→plan→code→commit loop reliable. Everything from here assumes you can steer Claude with
  memory and context.
- [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) (this repo's own) and [`meta/`](../../../meta/) — the living
  example of "facts live once, referenced not duplicated."
- The `@`-mention selection habit from [Explore a codebase](../02-explore-a-codebase/unit.md) is the
  context lever in miniature; the permission *postures* from [Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md)
  are configured in the `settings.json` discussed here.
- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where context/memory version-specifics
  are recorded if your CLI differs.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
</content>

3	---
4	id: U10
5	title: "Build a feature spec-first: requirements → design → tasks"
6	stage: force-multiplier
7	depth_tier: core
8	use_case: "Build a feature spec-first"
9	can_do: [C11]
10	workflows: [W7]
11	coverage_areas: [15]
12	prerequisites: [U5]
13	reading_time_min: 11
14	lab_time_min: 35
15	---
16	
17	# Build a feature spec-first: requirements → design → tasks
18	
19	## Learning objectives
20	
21	By the end of this unit you can:
22	
23	- **Run a spec-driven workflow for a feature whose cost-of-wrong is high** — drive Claude through
24	  **requirements → design → tasks** with an explicit approval gate between phases.
25	- **Write requirements that pin behavior, not implementation** — testable acceptance criteria with
26	  stable IDs, so "done" is objective and every later artifact has something to trace back to.
27	- **Hold the phase gates** — review and approve each phase *before* the next begins, rather than
28	  letting Claude race from a one-line prompt straight to code.
29	- **Keep the spec traceable** — every design choice serves a requirement, every task builds a design
30	  element, so you can answer "why does this code exist?" by walking back up the chain (cross-cutting verification, CV).
31	
32	## Fast path (TL;DR)
33	
34	> Spec-driven development ([spec-driven development](../../../meta/workflows.md#w7--spec-driven-development)) is the
35	> heavyweight cousin of **Ship a feature**'s explore→plan→code: for a feature where building the *wrong* thing is
36	> expensive, you align **before** writing code by producing three reviewed artifacts in order —
37	> **requirements** (the WHAT, as testable criteria), **design** (the HOW), **tasks** (the ordered
38	> build plan) — with an **approval gate** between each. The discipline is *gates and traceability*:
39	> don't start design until requirements are approved, and make every design choice and task trace
40	> back to a requirement. **This course's own [`specs/`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/) is the
41	> worked example** — you're reading a thing built this way. The lab has you run a mini spec for one
42	> small `taskflow-api` feature and grade it against a rubric (spec quality is a judgment call, so
43	> there's no automated verifier — the rubric *is* the skill).
44	
45	## Skip-check
46	
47	**Skip this unit if you can already:** take an ambiguous, higher-stakes feature and drive it
48	spec-first — write testable requirements with stable IDs, get them reviewed and **approved before
49	designing**, produce a design that traces to those requirements and a task plan that traces to the
50	design, and only then build — rather than jumping from a vague prompt to code and discovering the
51	requirements by arguing about the diff.
52	
53	## Concept
54	
55	**Ship a feature** taught the everyday loop: explore, plan, code, commit. Plan mode there is a *lightweight* gate —
56	one plan, reviewed, then you build. Spec-driven development is what you reach for when that's not
57	enough: a feature that's **large, ambiguous, or expensive to get wrong**, where the most costly
58	mistake isn't a bug, it's *building the wrong thing correctly.* The cure is to make alignment an
59	explicit, reviewable, phased artifact instead of an assumption buried in a prompt.
60	
61	**The workflow.** The generalized pattern lives once in
62	[`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w7--spec-driven-development); read it there. The
63	short version: **requirements → design → tasks, with an approval gate between phases, and each later
64	artifact traceable to the one before.** The rest of this section is what's specific to running it with
65	Claude.
66	
67	**1 — Requirements: pin the WHAT as testable criteria.** Have Claude help you write what the feature
68	must do — as **acceptance criteria you could turn into tests**, each with a **stable ID** — not how
69	it'll be coded. "The list endpoint SHALL exclude archived projects unless `include_archived=true`" is
70	a requirement; "add an `if` in the query builder" is not. The discipline that makes this pay off:
71	requirements describe *observable behavior and constraints*, and they get IDs (`R1`, `R2.AC3`…) so
72	that everything downstream can point at them. The single most common failure is letting an
73	implementation detail masquerade as a requirement — it pre-decides the design and smuggles in
74	assumptions no one reviewed.
75	
76	**2 — The gate is the point: review and approve before moving on.** Spec-driven development is only
77	worth the overhead if the gates are real. **Read the requirements and approve them before Claude
78	designs anything; read the design and approve it before it writes tasks.** An AI will happily produce
79	all three phases in one breath — and then you've reviewed nothing and bought none of the benefit. A
80	gate is a *decision*: redirect a wrong requirement while it's one sentence, not after it's load-bearing
81	in the design. (This course enforces exactly this rule on itself — requirements were approved before
82	design began, design before tasks.)
83	
84	**3 — Design and tasks: each must trace back.** The **design** says *how* — architecture, data shapes,
85	the pieces you'll build — and every meaningful choice should name the requirement it serves. The
86	**tasks** are the ordered, chunked build plan, each task implementing part of the design. Traceability
87	is the through-line: if a design element serves no requirement it's scope creep; if a requirement has
88	no design and no task, it's unbuilt. Being able to walk *requirement → design → task → code* (and back)
89	is what lets a reviewer — or future-you — answer "why does this exist?" without archaeology.
90	
91	**4 — Then build against the spec, and verify against it (CV).** With the spec approved, implementation
92	is the **Ship a feature** loop (explore→plan→code→commit) — but now the plan is largely written and the acceptance
93	criteria *are* your test list. Verification closes the loop back to the top: the change is done when it
94	satisfies the requirements you wrote, the tests mirror the acceptance criteria, and the traceability
95	holds. "It works" isn't the bar; "it does what `R-whatever` said, provably" is.
96	
97	> **A spec you didn't review is theater.** The entire value of spec-driven development is the
98	> *reviewed alignment* — three artifacts Claude generated and you rubber-stamped are worse than no
99	> spec, because they look like alignment without being it. Spend the attention at the gates; that's
100	> where the leverage is.
101	
102	**When NOT to reach for it.** Spec-driven is overhead, and overhead is only worth it when the cost of
103	building the wrong thing is high. A one-line fix or an obvious endpoint doesn't need three phases — use
104	**Ship a feature**'s plan mode. Reserve the full ceremony for genuinely ambiguous or expensive work; misapplying it to
105	trivial changes teaches your team that the process is bureaucracy.
106	
107	**Version currency.** Nothing in this unit depends on a version-specific Claude Code surface — spec-driven development is a
108	*method* (you can run it with files Claude reads and writes via the normal tools; some setups add spec
109	commands, but none are required). See [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) if
110	your CLI differs.
111	
112	## Worked example
113	
114	**You are reading a course built this way, and its spec is open for you to study.** Everything in
115	[`specs/claude-code-mastery/`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/) is the worked example:
116	
117	- [`requirements.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md) — the WHAT as **EARS-style**
118	  acceptance criteria with stable IDs (`R1`–`R15`, `R*.AC*`). Notice they describe behavior and
119	  constraints ("THE COURSE SHALL…"), and that the IDs are frozen so design and tasks can cite them.
120	- [`design.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md) — the HOW. Each section is tagged with the
121	  requirement it serves (e.g. *"§3 Workflow methods → `meta/workflows.md` [R3]"*) — that bracketed
122	  `[R…]` is traceability you can see.
123	- [`tasks.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md) — the ordered, chunked build plan (the very
124	  plan this unit is a task within).
125	- [`decisions.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md) — the *why*, including rejected
126	  alternatives, so a later session doesn't re-litigate settled calls.
127	
128	The gates were real: requirements were reviewed and **approved before** design started, design before
129	tasks — the same rule step 2 asks of you. And the repo's own [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) names
130	the spec as the source of truth and tells every session to read
131	[`IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md) first. Read these before the
132	lab: they're what a *good* small spec is scaled up from, and the shape you're imitating.
133	
134	## Lab
135	
136	> **This lab has no automated verifier.** Whether a requirement is well-formed, a design is traceable,
137	> or a gate was genuinely held are **judgment calls** no script can grade — and the point of
138	> spec-driven development is the reviewed alignment, not a passing test. So the self-check is an objective **rubric** you
139	> apply to your own spec; applying it *is* the skill. (Precedent: **Explore a codebase**, **Memory & context**, **Git & PR**.)
140	
141	**Goal:** run a **miniature spec-driven workflow** — requirements → design → tasks, with a real gate
142	between phases — for one small feature in `taskflow-api`, then build it against your spec.
143	
144	**The feature (deliberately a little ambiguous — that's why it earns a spec):** add **task
145	dependencies** — a task can be "blocked by" one or more other tasks, and a blocked task can't be marked
146	`done` until its blockers are done. The ambiguity is the point; your requirements have to *decide*
147	things like: What happens on a dependency cycle? Can a task depend on one in another project? What's
148	the error when you try to complete a blocked task? Does deleting a blocker unblock its dependents? None
149	of these has an obvious answer — which is exactly when spec-first beats jumping to code.
150	
151	**Steps:**
152	
153	1. **Requirements (then gate).** Have Claude draft acceptance criteria for the feature — testable,
154	   ID'd (`R1`, `R2`…), describing behavior not implementation, and resolving the ambiguities above.
155	   **Review them. Approve them out loud before continuing.** Redirect anything vague or
156	   implementation-flavored now.
157	2. **Design (then gate).** From the *approved* requirements, have Claude propose the design — the schema
158	   change (how dependencies are stored), the validation (cycle/cross-project rules), the service and
159	   route changes — with **each choice naming the requirement it serves**. Review for traceability and
160	   for the service-layer convention from [Ship a feature](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s `CLAUDE.md`. Approve.
161	3. **Tasks.** Have Claude break the design into an ordered task list, each task pointing at a design
162	   element. This is your build plan.
163	4. **Build against the spec.** Implement following the tasks with the **Ship a feature** loop
164	   (explore→plan→code→commit), using your acceptance criteria as the test list. Run `pytest`.
165	5. **Verify against the spec (CV).** Confirm each requirement is satisfied by a test, the design choices
166	   all trace to a requirement, and nothing was built that no requirement asked for.
167	
168	**Self-check (objective rubric):** your spec-driven run is sound when **all** hold:
169	
170	- [ ] Requirements are **testable acceptance criteria with stable IDs**, describing *behavior/
171	      constraints*, not implementation — and they **decide** the ambiguous cases (cycles, cross-project,
172	      the blocked-completion error, blocker deletion).
173	- [ ] The phases were produced **in order with a real gate** — you reviewed and approved requirements
174	      *before* design existed, and design *before* tasks. (You didn't generate all three at once.)
175	- [ ] **Traceability holds both ways:** every design choice and task names a requirement it serves, and
176	      every requirement has at least one design element + task. No orphans either direction.
177	- [ ] The implementation **satisfies the requirements**, with tests that mirror the acceptance criteria;
178	      `pytest` is green.
179	- [ ] Nothing was built that **no requirement asked for** (scope creep is visible as an untraceable
180	      diff).
181	
182	**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch — the artifact is *your* spec-and-process, and the rubric
183	is how you grade it. For the *shape* of each artifact, compare against this repo's own
184	[`requirements.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md) /
185	[`design.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md) /
186	[`tasks.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md) — scaled down, your three should rhyme with
187	those three.
188	
189	## Common pitfalls
190	
191	- **Generating all three phases in one shot.** The most common way to "do spec-driven development" and
192	  get none of its value: ask for requirements+design+tasks at once, skim, and start coding. You reviewed
193	  nothing. The gates are the product.
194	- **Requirements that are really implementation.** "Add a `blocked_by` column" pre-decides the design.
195	  Write the *behavior* ("a task SHALL NOT be completable while a blocker is open") and let design choose
196	  the column.
197	- **Untraceable design.** A design choice that serves no requirement is scope creep; a requirement with
198	  no design is unbuilt. If you can't draw the line both ways, the spec isn't done.
199	- **Spec-driving a trivial change.** Three phases for a one-line fix is bureaucracy that teaches people
200	  to resent the process. Match the ceremony to the cost-of-wrong; small/obvious → **Ship a feature**'s plan mode.
201	- **Letting the build silently diverge from the spec.** If implementation teaches you the spec was
202	  wrong (it happens), *update the spec* and re-approve — don't just drift, or the traceability you built
203	  becomes a lie.
204	
205	## Going deeper
206	
207	- **Next:** **Code & security review** is how a reviewed spec's implementation gets a final
208	  correctness/security pass before merge; together these three are the Force-Multiplier disciplines —
209	  rescue (refactor), build-right (spec), and gate-before-merge (review).
210	- [`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w7--spec-driven-development) — generalized spec-driven
211	  development, the heavyweight cousin of [Ship a feature](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s explore→plan→code.
212	- The whole [`specs/claude-code-mastery/`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/) tree — the dogfooded
213	  worked example; start at
214	  [`IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md), as every session here does.
215	- The phase-gate discipline is the same one in this repo's [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) working
216	  agreements; the build-against-spec step reuses [Ship a feature](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s loop.
217	- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
218	  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
219

tasks — the same rule step 2 asks of you. And the repo's own [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) names
the spec as the source of truth and tells every session to read
[`IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md) first. Read these before the
lab: they're what a *good* small spec is scaled up from, and the shape you're imitating.

## Lab

> **This lab has no automated verifier.** Whether a requirement is well-formed, a design is traceable,
> or a gate was genuinely held are **judgment calls** no script can grade — and the point of
> spec-driven development is the reviewed alignment, not a passing test. So the self-check is an objective **rubric** you
> apply to your own spec; applying it *is* the skill. (Precedent: **Explore a codebase**, **Memory & context**, **Git & PR**.)

**Goal:** run a **miniature spec-driven workflow** — requirements → design → tasks, with a real gate
between phases — for one small feature in `taskflow-api`, then build it against your spec.

**The feature (deliberately a little ambiguous — that's why it earns a spec):** add **task
dependencies** — a task can be "blocked by" one or more other tasks, and a blocked task can't be marked
`done` until its blockers are done. The ambiguity is the point; your requirements have to *decide*
things like: What happens on a dependency cycle? Can a task depend on one in another project? What's
the error when you try to complete a blocked task? Does deleting a blocker unblock its dependents? None
of these has an obvious answer — which is exactly when spec-first beats jumping to code.

**Steps:**

1. **Requirements (then gate).** Have Claude draft acceptance criteria for the feature — testable,
   ID'd (`R1`, `R2`…), describing behavior not implementation, and resolving the ambiguities above.
   **Review them. Approve them out loud before continuing.** Redirect anything vague or
   implementation-flavored now.
2. **Design (then gate).** From the *approved* requirements, have Claude propose the design — the schema
   change (how dependencies are stored), the validation (cycle/cross-project rules), the service and
   route changes — with **each choice naming the requirement it serves**. Review for traceability and
   for the service-layer convention from [Ship a feature](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s `CLAUDE.md`. Approve.
3. **Tasks.** Have Claude break the design into an ordered task list, each task pointing at a design
   element. This is your build plan.
4. **Build against the spec.** Implement following the tasks with the **Ship a feature** loop
   (explore→plan→code→commit), using your acceptance criteria as the test list. Run `pytest`.
5. **Verify against the spec (CV).** Confirm each requirement is satisfied by a test, the design choices
   all trace to a requirement, and nothing was built that no requirement asked for.

**Self-check (objective rubric):** your spec-driven run is sound when **all** hold:

- [ ] Requirements are **testable acceptance criteria with stable IDs**, describing *behavior/
      constraints*, not implementation — and they **decide** the ambiguous cases (cycles, cross-project,
      the blocked-completion error, blocker deletion).
- [ ] The phases were produced **in order with a real gate** — you reviewed and approved requirements
      *before* design existed, and design *before* tasks. (You didn't generate all three at once.)
- [ ] **Traceability holds both ways:** every design choice and task names a requirement it serves, and
      every requirement has at least one design element + task. No orphans either direction.
- [ ] The implementation **satisfies the requirements**, with tests that mirror the acceptance criteria;
      `pytest` is green.
- [ ] Nothing was built that **no requirement asked for** (scope creep is visible as an untraceable
      diff).

**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch — the artifact is *your* spec-and-process, and the rubric
is how you grade it. For the *shape* of each artifact, compare against this repo's own
[`requirements.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md) /
[`design.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md) /
[`tasks.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md) — scaled down, your three should rhyme with
those three.

## Common pitfalls

- **Generating all three phases in one shot.** The most common way to "do spec-driven development" and
  get none of its value: ask for requirements+design+tasks at once, skim, and start coding. You reviewed
  nothing. The gates are the product.
- **Requirements that are really implementation.** "Add a `blocked_by` column" pre-decides the design.
  Write the *behavior* ("a task SHALL NOT be completable while a blocker is open") and let design choose
  the column.
- **Untraceable design.** A design choice that serves no requirement is scope creep; a requirement with
  no design is unbuilt. If you can't draw the line both ways, the spec isn't done.
- **Spec-driving a trivial change.** Three phases for a one-line fix is bureaucracy that teaches people
  to resent the process. Match the ceremony to the cost-of-wrong; small/obvious → **Ship a feature**'s plan mode.
- **Letting the build silently diverge from the spec.** If implementation teaches you the spec was
  wrong (it happens), *update the spec* and re-approve — don't just drift, or the traceability you built
  becomes a lie.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** **Code & security review** is how a reviewed spec's implementation gets a final
  correctness/security pass before merge; together these three are the Force-Multiplier disciplines —
  rescue (refactor), build-right (spec), and gate-before-merge (review).
- [`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w7--spec-driven-development) — generalized spec-driven
  development, the heavyweight cousin of [Ship a feature](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s explore→plan→code.
- The whole [`specs/claude-code-mastery/`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/) tree — the dogfooded
  worked example; start at
  [`IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../../../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md), as every session here does.
- The phase-gate discipline is the same one in this repo's [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../CLAUDE.md) working
  agreements; the build-against-spec step reuses [Ship a feature](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s loop.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).

  [`meta/version-data.yaml`](../../../meta/version-data.yaml) — and rendered for reading as the
  [CLI reference](../../reference/cli-reference.md). Same "facts live once" discipline: need a flag this
  course didn't drill? Look there rather than guessing.

content.text:
**8.6 complete.** Both dogfood pointers landed in the *Going deeper* sections (lowest-disturbance spot, reading-time untouched), rendered, and `make check` is green.

**What changed** (4 files, +24 lines):
- `course/units/04-memory-and-context/unit.src.md` → **"The exhaustive CLI reference."** — a titled pointer to [`meta/cli-reference.json`](meta/cli-reference.json) as the machine single-source *sibling of `version-data.yaml`*, plus the rendered learner page. No `{{vd:…}}` glyph or `R#` code in prose (P7 house style).
- `course/units/10-spec-driven-dev/unit.src.md` → **"A post-v1 enhancement, built the same way."** — a callout that the CLI reference itself was built spec-first, linking `design.md` §12 and the P8 task plan as the requirements→design→tasks chain on a real incremental enhancement.
- The two generated `unit.md` siblings (via `make render`).
- Task file 8.6 checkboxes marked ✅.

**Verification:** `make check` → *all checks passed*. Links resolve, version-refs resolve, both pages re-render in sync. `reading_time_min` stays 9 (U4) / 11 (U10). The one `PEND` (R8 not yet referenced) is pre-existing and unrelated.

**One pre-existing oddity I noticed** (not mine, not 8.6 scope): `course/units/04-memory-and-context/unit.src.md` ends with a stray, unmatched `</content>` tag (line 198) that renders into the learner-facing `unit.md`. U10's source doesn't have it. Want me to strip it as a tiny drive-by fix, or leave it for a separate pass?

Per the working agreement I've **not committed**. Next up is **8.7** (dynamic requirement discovery in `check-traceability`) — which is now unblocked since R16/R17 are referenced by these unit pointers + the reference page. Want me to commit 8.6 first, or roll straight into 8.7?

content: Building P8's CLI reference: I finished 8.6 (added dogfood pointers in U4 and U10, make check green), uncommitted per the working agreement. Next: commit 8.6, then start 8.7 (dynamic requirement discovery in check-traceability). (disable recaps in /config)

**Commit `bc0a9a5`** — `P8 (8.6): dogfood pointers — U4→cli-reference.json, U10→spec-driven build [R16.AC7,R14.AC2]`. The pre-commit hook ran `make check` and passed; `git status` is empty.

What landed:
- **U4** *Going deeper* — titled pointer to `meta/cli-reference.json` (machine sibling of `version-data.yaml`) + the rendered CLI reference page.
- **U10** *Going deeper* — callout that the CLI reference was itself built spec-first (requirement → design §12 → P8 task plan → generator).
- Stray `</content>` tag stripped from U4's source.
- Both `unit.md` re-rendered; task file 8.6 marked ✅.

Note: the WSL console hit its "duplicate-batch" flakiness again this session — results lagged a turn, so I over-issued some read-only commands. No harm to the commit, but I'll keep batches to a single call going forward.

Next is **8.7 — dynamic requirement discovery**: switch `check-traceability` from the hardcoded `R1–R15` range to discovering `### Rn` headings from `requirements.md` (keeping the can-do set closed at C1–C17+CV), then confirm R16/R17 are discovered-and-referenced. This is the slice that makes the new requirements actually enforced. Want me to start on 8.7?

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""check-traceability (R13.AC5): the two-way traceability the spec promises.
3	
4	  A. Every requirement R1–R15 is referenced by >= 1 course artifact (meta/, course/, tools/,
5	     .claude/, .github/ — i.e. the built course, not the spec that defines the requirements).
6	  B. Every can-do (C1–C17 + CV) traces to >= 1 LAB and >= 1 capstone-rubric dimension.
7	
8	Parts B (and any not-yet-referenced requirement) depend on content authored in P5/P6. This check
9	is built before that content (so it guards as soon as content lands), therefore:
10	  - default mode: unmet items are reported as PENDING and do NOT fail the build;
11	  - `--strict` (the release / final Definition-of-Done gate): PENDING becomes FAIL.
12	
13	A lab is detected as a `## Lab` heading in a unit; the can-dos it exercises are the unit's
14	front-matter `can_do`. Rubric dimensions are lines in course/capstone/rubric.md tagged with the
15	can-do id they assess, e.g. `- [C6] ...` (convention consumed here; authored in P6).
16	"""
17	import os
18	import re
19	import sys
20	
21	sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
22	from _common import (  # noqa: E402
23	    COURSE, META, ROOT, Reporter, iter_text_files, load_yaml, parse_frontmatter, unit_files,
24	)
25	
26	REQ_RE = re.compile(r"\bR(?:1[0-5]|[1-9])\b")
27	CANDO_TAG_RE = re.compile(r"\b(C(?:1[0-7]|[1-9])|CV)\b")
28	
29	
30	def main(argv):
31	    strict = "--strict" in argv
32	    rep = Reporter("check-traceability")
33	    cap = load_yaml(META / "capability-map.yaml")
34	    cids = {c["id"] for c in cap["capabilities"]}
35	
36	    # A. requirement references across the built course
37	    scan_roots = [META, COURSE, ROOT / "tools", ROOT / ".claude", ROOT / ".github",
38	                  ROOT / "README.md", ROOT / "CLAUDE.md"]
39	    referenced = set()
40	    for root in scan_roots:
41	        files = [root] if root.is_file() else list(iter_text_files(root))
42	        for path in files:
43	            try:
44	                referenced |= set(REQ_RE.findall(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")))
45	            except (UnicodeDecodeError, IsADirectoryError):
46	                continue
47	    all_reqs = {f"R{i}" for i in range(1, 16)}
48	    missing_reqs = sorted(all_reqs - referenced, key=lambda r: int(r[1:]))
49	    if missing_reqs:
50	        rep.pending(f"requirements not yet referenced by a course artifact: {missing_reqs}")
51	    else:
52	        rep.ok("every requirement R1–R15 is referenced by a course artifact")
53	
54	    # B. can-do -> lab and can-do -> rubric dimension
55	    lab_cands = set()
56	    for path in unit_files():
57	        fm = parse_frontmatter(path)
58	        if fm and "## Lab" in path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"):
59	            lab_cands |= set(fm.get("can_do", []))
60	    rubric = COURSE / "capstone" / "rubric.md"
61	    rubric_cands = set()
62	    if rubric.exists():
63	        rubric_cands = set(CANDO_TAG_RE.findall(rubric.read_text(encoding="utf-8")))
64	
65	    no_lab = sorted(cids - lab_cands)
66	    no_rubric = sorted(cids - rubric_cands)
67	    if no_lab:
68	        rep.pending(f"can-do(s) not yet practiced by a lab: {no_lab}")
69	    else:
70	        rep.ok("every can-do is practiced by >=1 lab")
71	    if no_rubric:
72	        rep.pending(f"can-do(s) not yet assessed by a rubric dimension: {no_rubric}")
73	    else:
74	        rep.ok("every can-do is assessed by >=1 capstone-rubric dimension")
75	
76	    return rep.done(strict=strict)
77	
78	
79	if __name__ == "__main__":
80	    raise SystemExit(main(sys.argv[1:]))
81

66	    no_rubric = sorted(cids - rubric_cands)
67	    if no_lab:
68	        rep.pending(f"can-do(s) not yet practiced by a lab: {no_lab}")
69	    else:
70	        rep.ok("every can-do is practiced by >=1 lab")
71	    if no_rubric:
72	        rep.pending(f"can-do(s) not yet assessed by a rubric dimension: {no_rubric}")
73	    else:
74	        rep.ok("every can-do is assessed by >=1 capstone-rubric dimension")
75	
76	    return rep.done(strict=strict)
77	
78	
79	if __name__ == "__main__":
80	    raise SystemExit(main(sys.argv[1:]))

content.content:
1:# Requirements — Claude Code Mastery Course
11:## 1. Introduction
13:### 1.1 Purpose
16:### 1.2 Product summary
25:### 1.3 Scope
30:### 1.4 Target persona
33:### 1.5 Definition of "elite" (the bar this course must clear)
38:## 2. Definitions & conventions
61:## 3. Functional requirements
63:### R1 — Capability map (outcome-based, non-ranking)
69:- R1.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL express progress as a **capability map of "can-do" statements** — outcome-phrased, observable capabilities ("I can …") that describe the work, never the learner.
70:- R1.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL group can-do statements into neutral **stages** named for the relationship with the tool (**First Wins → Daily Driver → Force Multiplier → Autonomy & Scale**), used only for shape and orientation, NOT as a ranking of the learner.
71:- R1.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL NOT use person-ranking labels (e.g., novice/beginner/intermediate/advanced/elite) in learner-facing material to describe the learner's level.
72:- R1.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL map every unit to exactly one stage and to the specific can-do statements it advances, stated in the unit's front matter.
73:- R1.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL ensure every can-do statement is (a) practiced by at least one lab and (b) assessed by at least one dimension of the capstone rubric.
74:- R1.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL state, in the top-level README, the entry capabilities assumed and the full set of can-do statements the course delivers.
76:### R2 — Use-case-driven curriculum (primary organizing axis)
80:- R2.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL be organized primarily around real-world use cases (jobs-to-be-done), with each unit centered on a realistic scenario and its outcome — NOT around a feature checklist.
81:- R2.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL maintain a prioritized **use-case catalog** selected primarily by *frequency × leverage* (most common and most powerful first) and secondarily by *capability-map coverage* (the catalog SHALL collectively exercise every can-do statement in R1 and every capability area in R4), recording the prioritization rationale.
82:- R2.AC3 — WHEN a use case is taught, THE COURSE SHALL state: the job, the realistic scenario, the definition of a successful outcome, and the workflow(s) and feature(s) it employs.
83:- R2.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL ground each use case in real, observed engineering practice (a documented or industry-recognized workflow), NOT in a contrived or invented scenario, and SHALL be able to cite the practice it reflects.
84:- R2.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL give the most common and most powerful use cases full depth, WHERE rarer use cases MAY be covered at awareness level (see R5).
85:- R2.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL treat the concrete use-case catalog as a Design deliverable; this requirement fixes the *selection method*, not the specific list.
87:### R3 — Workflow methods
91:- R3.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL teach, applied within use cases (not in isolation), at minimum these workflow patterns, each tagged with the stage at which it is introduced (final stage assignments confirmed in Design against the capability map):
101:- R3.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL present spec-driven development as a first-class workflow and SHALL use this very course's spec (`requirements.md`/`design.md`/`tasks.md`) as the worked example.
102:- R3.AC3 — WHEN a workflow is taught, THE COURSE SHALL state its decision criteria (when to choose it over alternatives) and its expected verification step.
103:- R3.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL introduce each workflow in the context of a use case that needs it, then generalize the pattern.
104:- R3.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL map every named workflow to the specific use case(s) that exercise it, so no workflow is taught as a free-floating abstract lesson; a workflow with no exercising use case SHALL be treated as a gap to resolve.
105:- R3.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL reconcile this workflow list against the Design use-case catalog (R2.AC6), resolving any item that is better expressed as a use case rather than a workflow, or vice versa.
107:### R4 — Feature coverage guarantee (secondary; tiered)
111:- R4.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL maintain a **feature-coverage matrix** as the single canonical source of truth for the set of Claude Code capability areas the course tracks; the matrix SHALL be the authoritative list (this requirement deliberately does NOT duplicate the enumerated list inline, supporting version resilience per R12). The matrix SHALL be created/seeded in Design and kept current per the R12 refresh process.
112:- R4.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL ensure every capability area in the matrix is covered somewhere in the course at a depth appropriate to its tier (see R4.AC4), and the matrix SHALL map each area to the unit(s)/example(s)/lab(s) that include it and to its depth tier.
113:- R4.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL teach features in service of use cases — never as a standalone checklist — and SHALL state, for each high-leverage feature, when/why to use it plus at least one anti-pattern or failure mode.
114:- R4.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL tier feature coverage by *frequency × leverage* (mirroring R2): **high-leverage** capabilities SHALL be covered by at least one hands-on lab; **awareness-level** capabilities MAY be covered by at least one worked example or mention (named, shown once, with pointers). Each capability's tier SHALL be recorded in the matrix; tier assignments are a Design deliverable.
115:- R4.AC5 — IF a capability is intentionally excluded, THEN THE COURSE SHALL record the exclusion and its rationale in the matrix rather than omitting it silently.
117:### R5 — Tiered depth & pacing for experienced engineers
121:- R5.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL assume an experienced, fast-learning software engineer and SHALL NOT teach *general software-engineering* basics (general programming, Git fundamentals, terminal basics). This exclusion SHALL NOT extend to AI-assisted-development and Claude Code concepts (e.g., context windows, non-determinism, the agentic loop, verification discipline, tool/permission models), which are **core content** the persona is new to; first-run onboarding/setup is covered by R11.
122:- R5.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL give every unit a **fast path** (a concise TL;DR/speedrun: the essential commands plus the lab) distinct from the full exposition, so a confident reader can skim then practice.
123:- R5.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL give every unit an optional **skip-check**: a brief "you can skip this unit if you can already …" self-assessment phrased as can-do statements (R1), so an experienced reader can decide whether to skip the unit entirely.
124:- R5.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL tier depth explicitly: core/high-leverage material is deep and hands-on; awareness material is brief and clearly labeled as such.
125:- R5.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL teach each concept once in its home unit and reference it elsewhere; WHERE independent readability (R6.AC3) requires context from another unit, a one-line recap plus a reference is permitted, but full re-explanation is not.
126:- R5.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL provide, per unit, two separate time estimates — **reading time** and **lab time** — so a learner can budget and skip deliberately.
128:### R6 — Unit content standard
132:- R6.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL require every **core-tier** unit to contain, in order: front matter; learning objectives; a fast-path/TL;DR; a skip-check (R5.AC3); concept exposition; at least one worked example; at least one lab; a "common pitfalls" section; and a "going deeper" pointer set.
133:- R6.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL allow **awareness-tier** units a reduced template: front matter; learning objectives; at least one worked example or mention; and a "going deeper" pointer set (fast-path, skip-check, lab, and pitfalls OPTIONAL). The required template per unit SHALL follow the unit's depth tier (R4.AC4 / R5.AC4).
134:- R6.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL provide every unit machine-readable front matter conforming to a defined schema (keys for: title, stage, can-do statements advanced, use case, prerequisites, reading-time estimate, lab-time estimate, depth tier); the exact schema SHALL be defined in Design and SHALL be lintable (supports R13.AC4).
135:- R6.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL state learning objectives as observable behaviors (action verbs), not topics, and SHALL map each objective to the specific can-do statement(s) (R1) it advances.
136:- R6.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL keep each unit independently readable, declaring any cross-unit dependency explicitly in front matter; per R5.AC5, independent readability is satisfied by a one-line recap plus a reference, not by re-teaching.
137:- R6.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL scope the "common pitfalls" section to *learner mistakes when performing this unit's task*; this is distinct from feature misuse/anti-patterns (R4.AC3) and from safety hazards (R10), which are covered by their respective requirements.
